front: Work: This Pound poem begins 'And then went down to the ship,' adapting the nekuia from the Odyssey as its first section.
Work: In the second section of this poem, the speaker addresses Robert Browning: 'Hang it all, there can be but the one Sordello.'
Work: Canto XLV of this poem repeats the phrase 'with usura' over twenty times, denouncing the practice as something that 'rusteth the chisel.'
Work: This poem contains sections named for China, John Adams, 'Rock-Drill,' 'Thrones,' and Pisa, and in its later sections incorporates Chinese characters.
Work: This poem contains the line 'what thou lovest well remains / the rest is dross' and the Modernist dictum 'Make it new.'
Work: This poem controversially won the first Bollingen Prize, causing the Library of Congress to withdraw its involvement.
Work: one section of this poem draws heavily from Thomas Hart Benton
Work: A Brazilian group of concrete poets called Noigandres took their name from a passage in this long poem.
Work: This subsection of The Cantos was written while Pound was imprisoned in an exposed cage near Pisa, partly on toilet paper.
Work: The 81st poem of this sequence repeats the line 'pull down thy vanity,' while the 80th ends 'God knows what else is left of London, our London.'
Work: the first poem of this section adopts the persona of 'OY TIS' and later blends into Wuluwaid and Ouan Jin
Work: Pound shared a prison cell with Louis Till (Emmett Till's father) while writing this section of The Cantos.
Work: This two-line Imagist poem reads in its entirety: 'The apparition of these faces in the crowd: / Petals on a wet, black bough.'
back: In a Station of the Metro (Ezra Pound)
work: In a Station of the Metro

front: Work: Pound reportedly reduced this poem from thirty lines to just fourteen words, and it is sometimes called the first American haiku.
back: In a Station of the Metro (Ezra Pound)
work: In a Station of the Metro

front: Work: In a 1914 essay about Vorticism, Pound described how the scene inspiring this poem connected to Kandinsky's Concerning the Spiritual in Art.
back: In a Station of the Metro (Ezra Pound)
work: In a Station of the Metro

front: Work: The title character of this Pound poem 'strove to resuscitate the dead art of poetry,' and his 'true Penelope was Flaubert.'
Work: This poem laments a 'botched civilization' and 'an old bitch gone in the teeth,' stating 'the age demanded an image of its accelerated grimace.'
Work: This poem contains sections called 'Yeux Glauques,' 'Brennbaum,' and 'Mr. Nixon,' in which Mr. Nixon tells the speaker to 'give up verse, my boy.'
Work: This Pound poem ends with 'Medallion' and includes an 'Envoi' section that mimics Edmund Waller's 'Go Lovely Rose.'
Work: This Pound collection of loosely translated Chinese poems includes 'The River Merchant's Wife: A Letter' and 'Jewel Stairs' Grievance,' based on Li Bai.
Work: In the most famous poem from Cathay, the speaker promises 'And I will come out to meet you / As far as Cho-Fu-Sa.'
back: The River Merchant's Wife: A Letter (Ezra Pound / Cathay)

front: Work: Pound published the essay 'A Few Don'ts by an Imagiste,' which warns 'don't chop your stuff into separate iambs' and 'don't be viewy.'
back: A Few Don'ts by an Imagiste (Ezra Pound)

front: Work: Pound collaborated with H.D. on three principles of this movement: 'direct treatment of the thing,' 'no superfluous words,' and using musical rhythm.
Author: this poet's study of Chinese characters influenced his development of Imagism
Work: Pound co-founded this art movement with Wyndham Lewis and published its manifesto in the magazine BLAST.
Work: In an essay coining the name of this movement, Pound defined its namesake concept as 'the point of maximum energy.'
Author: this poet's wife, Dorothy Shakespear, published paintings in the magazine BLAST
Work: This Pound critical work defined great literature as 'language charged with meaning to the utmost possible degree' and literature as 'news that STAYS news.'
Work: This Pound critical work opens with the 'parable of the sunfish,' in which Louis Agassiz repeatedly forces a student to describe a sunfish.
Work: In this essay, Pound propounded his theory of the 'luminous detail' and compared the poet's task to that of the goddess Isis.
back: I Gather the Limbs of Osiris (Ezra Pound)

front: Work: this Pound critical work discusses troubadour poetry
Author: wrote that 'any general statement is like a cheque drawn on a bank' whose 'value depends on what is there to meet it'
Author: This poet broadcast fascist propaganda from Italy during WWII, was captured and detained in a cage near Pisa, and was charged with treason.
Author: After pleading insanity on treason charges, this poet was confined to St. Elizabeths Hospital for over a decade.
Author: T.S. Eliot dedicated The Waste Land to this poet as 'il miglior fabbro,' borrowing a phrase Dante applied to Arnaut Daniel.
Author: This poet convinced T.S. Eliot to cut sections from a poem originally titled 'He Do the Police in Different Voices,' resulting in The Waste Land.
Author: Elizabeth Bishop wrote 'Visits to St. Elizabeths' about visiting this incarcerated poet, using a cumulative nursery-rhyme pattern.
Author: This poet organized the 'Bel Esprit' proto-crowdfunding initiative to financially support T.S. Eliot.
Author: In Bob Dylan's 'Desolation Row,' 'calypso singers laugh at' this poet and T.S. Eliot as they fight 'in the captain's tower.'
Author: A Robert Lowell sonnet describes this poet looking at his 'blotched, bent hands' and offering the one-word observation, 'Worms.'
Author: Daniel Bell led opposition to awarding this poet the Emerson-Thoreau Medal, arguing art could not be separated from morality.
Author: translated 'The Seafarer' from Old English
Author: wrote music reviews under the pseudonym 'William Atheling' and championed George Antheil in a Treatise on Harmony
Author: with his lover, the violinist Olga Rudge, this poet helped repopularize Vivaldi's music
Work: This Pound poem parodies 'Sumer is icumen in' as 'Winter is icumen in, Lhude sing Goddamm.'
Work: In 'Ballad of the Goodly Fere,' Simon Zealot declares 'If they think they ha' slain our Goodly Fere they are fools eternally.'
back: Ballad of the Goodly Fere (Ezra Pound)

front: Work: Pound's only opera, containing the aria 'Dictes Moy,' is based on the work of a medieval French poet.
Author: This poet suggested H.D. publish under her initials and was also her lover.
Author: This poet and T.S. Eliot gave each other animal nicknames (Rabbit and Possum) inspired by the Uncle Remus stories.
Work: Pound wrote an essay about economics titled 'Oro e Lavoro' ('Gold and Work') while living under the Salo Republic in Italy.
